items.title: Sudden painless monocular visual loss
items.contentHtml:
Monocular sudden painless visual loss

  • Monocular sudden painless visual loss is the classic presentation of central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO).
  • The loss is profound, often at the level of light perception or non-light perception.
  • The mechanism is acute retinal ischemia due to arterial flow obstruction; The retina has a high oxygen demand and does not tolerate more than 90 minutes of complete ischemia.
  • The main differential diagnosis is anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION), which is usually painful on ocular mobilization and associated with giant cell arteritis.
  • Typical question: 65-year-old patient with sudden onset painless monocular visual loss; The first diagnostic maneuver is direct ophthalmoscopy.

items.title: Ophthalmoscopy: cherry red macule
items.contentHtml:
Ophthalmoscopy: cherry red macula

  • The cherry red macula is the pathognomonic finding of occlusion of the central retinal artery in ophthalmoscopy.
  • It occurs because the edematous and whitish peripheral retina contrasts with the fovea, which, being avascular and thinner, allows the underlying red choroid to be seen.
  • This sign appears typically 1 to 2 hours after occlusion and persists for weeks.
  • It should not be confused with the cherry red spot of lysosomal storage diseases (for example, Tay-Sachs disease), which is bilateral and progressive.
  • In OACR, the cherry red macule is unilateral and acute.

items.title: Arteriolar narrowing and retinal edema
items.contentHtml:
Arteriolar narrowing and retinal edema

  • In central retinal artery occlusion, ophthalmoscopy shows diffuse arteriolar narrowing and whitish retinal edema in the posterior pole.
  • retinal edema is most evident in the inner retina, which is the layer supplied by the central artery of the retina.
  • The arterioles can be seen as fine white lines (silver threads) due to luminal collapse and lack of flow.
  • The peripheral retina may be spared if there is collateral circulation from the choroid.
  • The absence of arteriolar narrowing should raise the suspicion of branch occlusion or a different etiology, such as optic neuropathy ischemic.

items.title: Relative afferent pupillary defect
items.contentHtml:
Relative afferent pupillary defect

  • The relative afferent pupillary defect (RPD) is a key clinical finding in central retinal artery occlusion.
  • It is evaluated with the swinging flashlight test: in the affected eye, the pupil paradoxically dilates when illuminated (response consensual < direct).
  • DPAR indicates extensive optic nerve or retinal injury; in CRAO it is present because retinal ischemia affects the afferent pathway.
  • A negative DPAR does not rule out CRAO if the occlusion is incomplete or branch occlusion.
  • The presence of DPAR helps to differentiate CRAO from causes of visual loss due to media opacity (cataract, vitreous hemorrhage), which do not produce DPAR.

items.foco: Embolism as the most common cause
items.title: Embolism as the most common cause
items.contentHtml:
Embolism as the most common cause

  • Embolism is the most common cause of occlusion of the central retinal artery, representing approximately 80% of cases.
  • The most common emboli are of carotid origin (atheromatous plaques) or cardiac (atrial fibrillation, heart valves). prosthetics, endocarditis).
  • Cholesterol emboli (Hollenhorst emboli) are shiny and lodge in arteriolar bifurcations; those of fibrin-platelets are grayish.
  • In patients < 40 years, consider other causes such as retinal vasculitis (for example, lupus, antiphospholipid syndrome) or retinal migraine.
  • Identification of the embolus on ophthalmoscopy guides the embolic source and management.

items.foco: Giant cell arteritis in the elderly
items.title: Giant cell arteritis in the elderly
items.contentHtml:
Giant cell arteritis in the elderly

  • Giant cell arteritis (GCA) is an important cause of central retinal artery occlusion in patients > 50 years, especially > 70.
  • It is associated with systemic symptoms: temporary headache, jaw claudication, polymyalgia rheumatica, fever, weight loss.
  • The ESR and C-reactive protein are very high; Temporal artery biopsy is the gold standard.
  • Urgent treatment is intravenous corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3 days) to prevent involvement of the other eye.
  • In the event of OACR in an elderly person, always ask about GCA symptoms; If there is suspicion, start corticosteroids without waiting for the biopsy.

items.title: Fundus: cherry red spot
items.contentHtml:
Fundox: cherry red spot

  • The cherry red spot in the fundus is the hallmark sign of central retinal artery occlusion.
  • It appears as a red area in the fovea surrounded by whitish edematous retina.
  • The cherry red spot is not immediately visible; It typically develops after 1-2 hours of occlusion.
  • In OACR the cherry red spot is unilateral; in storage diseases such as Tay-Sachs disease it is bilateral.
  • The presence of cherry red spot confirms the diagnosis of CRAO and excludes other causes of sudden visual loss such as ischemic optic neuropathy.

items.title: Occlusion secondary to atrial fibrillation
items.contentHtml:
Occlusion secondary to atrial fibrillation

  • Atrial fibrillation is a common cardiac cause of occlusion of the central retinal artery due to embolism.
  • Embolli formed in the left atrium (especially in the appendage) travel through the carotid circulation to the ophthalmic artery.
  • The risk is greater in non-valvular atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc > 2; oral anticoagulation reduces the risk of stroke and CRAO.
  • In all patients with CRAO without another obvious cause, an electrocardiogram and Holter monitoring should be performed to detect paroxysmal atrial fibrillation.
  • Treatment of atrial fibrillation with oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban, warfarin) is the main preventive measure to avoid recurrences.

items.title: Retinal vascularitis in young people
items.contentHtml:
Retinal vascularitis in young people

  • In young patients (< 40 years) with occlusion of the central retinal artery, retinal vascularitis is a relevant cause.
  • It can be associated with autoimmune diseases: systemic lupus erythematosus, antiphospholipid syndrome, Behçet's disease, sarcoidosis.
  • Also consider primary vasculitis of the central nervous system or infections such as toxoplasmosis or syphilis.
  • The study includes antinuclear antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipins, ESR, PCR and serologies.
  • Treatment is directed at the underlying cause: immunosuppressants (corticosteroids, cyclophosphamide) in autoimmune vasculitis; antibiotics in infections.

items.title: Treatment: eye massage and paracentesis
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: eye massage and paracentesis

  • Acute treatment of central retinal artery occlusion includes eye massage and anterior chamber paracentesis to reduce intraocular pressure and dislodge the embolus.
  • Eye massage: intermittent pressure is applied to the closed globe for 10-15 seconds, followed by sudden release, repeating several times.
  • Paracentesis: 0.1-0.2 mL of aqueous humor is extracted to reduce IOP to < 10 mmHg and promote retinal perfusion.
  • Other measures: carbogen inhalation (95% O2 + 5% CO2) to vasodilate and increase retinal oxygenation; acetazolamide 500 mg intravenously to reduce IOP.
  • Therapeutic window is < 6-12 hours, although the visual prognosis remains poor even with treatment.

items.title: Generally poor visual prognosis
items.contentHtml:
Generally poor visual prognosis

  • The generally poor visual prognosis of central retinal artery occlusion is due to the high vulnerability of the retina to ischemia.
  • Less than 20% of patients recover useful vision (> 20/200) even with acute treatment.
  • Spontaneous recovery is rare and is associated with occlusions incomplete or with cilioretinal circulation present.
  • The presence of a visible embolus on ophthalmoscopy does not modify the prognosis; The duration of ischemia does (> 90 minutes produces irreversible damage).
  • The objective of management is to prevent recurrences and major vascular events (stroke, heart attack) by controlling risk factors and antithrombotic treatment.

items.title: Evaluate carotid and transthoracic echocardiogram
items.contentHtml:
Evaluate carotid and transthoracic echocardiogram

  • In the event of occlusion of the central retinal artery, it is mandatory to evaluate the embolic source by carotid Doppler ultrasound and transthoracic echocardiogram.
  • The carotid Doppler looks for stenosis > 50% or ulcerated plaques; if detected, consider carotid endarterectomy or stent.
  • The transthoracic echocardiogram evaluates intracavitary thrombi, valvulopathies and patent foramen ovale; if negative, transesophageal echocardiogram may be indicated.
  • In patients with known atrial fibrillation, transthoracic echocardiogram evaluates atrial size and ventricular function.
  • Etiological study reduces the risk of future events by identifying and treating the underlying cause.
